title: Locus of control, educational attainment, and college aspirations: the relative role of effort and expectations
abstract: We study the relationship between locus of control and both educational attainment and college aspirations in adolescence, focusing on the potential channels through which locus of control may influence these outcomes. Even after controlling for a comprehensive set of socio-demographic factors and cognitive skills, there remains a strong correlation between locus of control, college aspirations, and attendance. We show that effort is an essential channel through which locus of control operates. Mediation analysis further reveals that this mechanism is more influential than future expectations in determining high-school graduation, college aspirations, and attendance.
summary_en: Locus of control — the belief that outcomes depend on one's own actions — matters for education. This paper analyses the relationship between locus of control and educational attainment plus college aspirations in adolescents. Even after controlling for socio-demographic factors and cognitive skills, locus of control strongly correlates with college aspirations and attendance. Mediation analysis reveals the key channel: effort. Expectations matter too but less. The result is sharper than previous work because it identifies the specific mechanism through which locus of control operates.
data_used: Survey data on adolescent locus of control, cognitive skills, socio-demographic characteristics, college aspirations, and attainment.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian education and social-mobility policy (Oktatási Hivatal, EMMI). The paper supports non-cognitive-skill interventions (specifically targeting effort, not just expectations) to improve educational outcomes for at-risk students. Relevant to Tanoda-type programmes and career-counselling design. Caveat: correlational identification; the causal effect of locus-of-control interventions needs experimental evidence.
